questions.question: What two motions occur when an engine fails on a multi-engine airplane?
questions.explanation: When an engine fails, two motions occur: YAW (caused by asymmetrical thrust toward the inoperative engine) and ROLL (caused by the yawing moment creating unequal airspeed over the wings and asymmetric lift from propeller slipstream).

questions.question: On a conventional twin with clockwise-rotating propellers, which engine is the critical engine?
questions.explanation: On a conventional twin with clockwise-rotating propellers, the left engine is the critical engine. Due to P-factor, the descending blade on the right engine has a longer arm from the C.G., creating greater yaw when the left engine fails.

questions.question: Which engine is the critical engine on the DA42-VI?

options.text: Neither — it has no critical engine

questions.explanation: The DA42-VI is a conventional twin with both propellers rotating clockwise. The left engine is the critical engine because losing it leaves the right engine, which has a longer P-factor thrust arm, creating a greater yawing moment.

questions.question: What are the three factors that determine the critical engine?
questions.explanation: The three factors that determine the critical engine are: 1) P-factor (asymmetric thrust from descending blade), 2) Accelerated slipstream (asymmetric lift from propeller wash), and 3) Torque (reaction force from engine rotation).

questions.question: How does torque affect the airplane when the left engine fails on a conventional twin?

options.text: Torque opposes the yaw from the right engine, reducing the total effect
Torque works together with thrust to yaw and roll the airplane to the left
Torque has no effect on yaw or roll

questions.explanation: If the left engine fails on a conventional twin, the yawing moment from the right engine thrust and the total torque both work together to yaw and roll the airplane to the left, making the effect worse than a right engine failure.
